title: How to Navigate Changing Friendships and Relationship Shifts: A Practical Guide for Relationship Transitions
author: Alice Kim, LICSW
content:
Life is constantly changing, and so are our relationships. Whether you are navigating a painful breakup, deepening a commitment, adjusting to a relocation, or simply noticing that a friendship is drifting, relationship changes are a natural part of the human experience.

It is normal to feel distress when these shifts happen. Change challenges our fundamental human need for emotional stability and predictability. However, when we understand that relationships have ebbs and flows—and that we can have different levels of closeness across different seasons—we can relieve the pressure to maintain the same level of connection with everyone indefinitely.

Having a clear framework can help normalize and provide perspective on this changing dynamic. One tool for understanding these changes is the Concentric Circles of Relationships model, paired with insights from sociological research on human social networks.

Understanding the Framework: Concentric Circles and Dunbar's Number

This concept stems from research by anthropologist Robin Dunbar, who studied how many close relationships the human brain can realistically maintain. The concentric circles framework visualizes our social world as a series of rings radiating outward from the self.

Think of your social world as a target, with you in the middle. Research suggests our brains have the capacity to keep up with about 150 stable relationships at any given time. These relationships spread out in rings based on the level of intimacy.

  • The Center (You): The individual.
  • Ring 1: Intimate/Inner Circle (approx. 5 or less): Partners, primary attachment figures, or closest confidants. These relationships involve high vulnerability, deep emotional reliance, and mutual support.
  • Ring 2: Good Friends and Trusted Community (approx. 15 or less): Close friends, trusted family members, and regular social support. These involve shared activities and consistent contact, but slightly lower intensity than the inner circle.
  • Ring 3: Friends, Meaningful Contacts, and Colleagues (50–150): Coworkers, casual acquaintances, and neighbors. Interactions here are typically bound by context and shared environment.
  • Ring 4: Community and Professional Contacts (500): Acquaintances, service providers, and public figures. Interactions are functional and brief.

When life transitions occur, we must allocate our limited time and emotional energy across these social rings. When new people enter our lives, we need to redistribute that energy, determine where they fit, and create space if necessary.

Why Relationship Transitions Hurt

Transitions usually involve movement between these circles. For example:

  • A romantic partner moving from Ring 1 to Ring 3 or exiting the social network entirely following a breakup.
  • A casual coworker moving from Ring 3 into Ring 2 as a professional relationship blossoms into a genuine friendship.
  • A close friend pulling back due to life changes, moving from Ring 1 to Ring 3 during a busy season, with the potential to move back toward Ring 2 or 1 later.

Distress occurs when our emotional investment and expectations do not match the current ring a person occupies. Experiencing grief or anxiety during these shifts is a normal response to the change in your social ecosystem.

Coping Strategies Grounded in Clinical Frameworks

When managing the stress of changing relationships, these steps can help regain understanding and agency:

1. Audit Your Circles

Take a moment to draw four concentric circles on a piece of paper. Place the key people in your life into their current rings based on current reality, rather than history or future potential. Ask yourself: Where does this person actually sit today in terms of mutual investment, communication, and support? Recognizing reality reduces the friction of trying to force a relationship to stay in a ring it has outgrown.

2. Reallocate Emotional Resources

Emotional resources require energy, and energy is a limited resource. If a relationship has shifted outward (e.g., from Ring 1 to Ring 3), continuing to invest Ring 1 emotional energy into it will lead to burnout and resentment when the other is putting in Ring 3 energy. Consciously redirect that energy and emotional resources to mutual relationships, community investment, or self-care.

3. Establish Behavioral Boundaries

Transitions often blur lines. If a relationship is changing, decide what level of contact supports your well-being.

  • Internal Boundary: If you are transitioning from close friends to more distant acquaintances, a behavioral boundary might look like muting their stories on social media to stop checking in constantly, or deciding not to text at the same frequency. You are not doing this to freeze them out; you are setting a rule for yourself that preserves your mental energy.
  • External Boundary: If you need to establish a verbal boundary, keep it focused on your capacity. Instead of saying, "You text me too much," try: "I am going through a demanding season right now and am pulling back from my phone in the evenings to recharge. If I don't reply right away, please know I care about you, and I won't be answering messages past 8 PM."

4. Practice Radical Acceptance

Using principles from DBT and ACT, acknowledge the transition without fighting the reality of it. Instead of ruminating on why a phase had to end, radical acceptance sounds like: "I wish this transition hadn't happened, and fighting it only keeps me stuck in pain. It is happening, and my work now is to align my actions with my values. What am I willing to do that aligns with my values and what relationships are available to me right now that are enjoyable and meaningful?”

Moving Forward

Relationship transitions require ongoing calibration of our social boundaries and emotional investments. By visualizing where people belong in your life and understanding the natural ebb and flow of social seasons, you can align your expectations with reality, protect your energy, and navigate change with greater stability.

References:

  • https://www.bbc.com/future/article/20191001-dunbars-number-why-we-can-only-maintain-150-relationships

If you find that relational transitions are taking up your time and energy and need a partner to process, partnering with a life transitions coach or licensed mental health professional can provide personalized support and specialized tools.

Ready to Begin?

title: Do I Need Therapy? Understanding How Counseling Can Help with Anxiety, Depression, Stress, & Burnout
author: Alice Kim, LICSW
content:
Life is complex. Sometimes, the challenges you’re facing run deeper than a transition or a temporary "rough patch." You might notice that you aren’t quite feeling like yourself, or perhaps the ways you’ve always managed your stress aren't working anymore and it is affecting your day to day.

When you feel overwhelmed, emotionally stuck, or weighed down by patterns that seem challenging to break, counseling can be useful. Professional counseling is a collaborative therapeutic relationship to help improve your overall wellbeing. It focuses on understanding your symptoms, looking beneath the surface to see why you’re feeling stuck, and developing the tools toward healing.

Is This the Right Path for Me?

Counseling often focuses on "what’s happening now" and "how we got here." You might find counseling to be helpful if you are experiencing:

  • Persistent Anxiety: A constant hum of worry that interferes with your sleep, work, or relationships.
  • Depression: More than just sadness, it is a persistent heaviness that saps your energy, motivation, and sense of connection to the things you used to enjoy.
  • Overwhelming Stress: Feeling like you're "running on empty" and struggling to keep up with the demands of daily life.
  • Burnout: When the demands of work, caregiving, or life leave you emotionally and physically depleted.
  • Relationship Challenges: Repeating the same arguments, feeling misunderstood, or struggling to set or maintain boundaries.
  • Your Past Affecting your Present: Recognizing that old patterns or experiences are still influencing how you show up in your present-day life.

What does a counseling session look like?

  • Developing a safe and trusted relationship to understand the whole person, not just the issue.
  • Making sense of the stuck places: Exploring the stressors, emotional loops and patterns, that may be shaping your day-to-day life.
  • Working at a pace that feels grounded and not rushed. It can move thoughtfully, with compassion and clear direction.
  • Building tools you can actually use: Process and insight is the starting point. Learning practical strategies and skills to support you outside the sessions helps foster change and growth.
  • Supporting lasting healing: The goal is not perfection or to be "fixed". The goal is helping you feel more connected, more resilient, and better able to meet life as it is.

How long does the counseling process take?

There is no one-size-fits-all timeline. Some clients find what they need after a few sessions, while others value the ongoing support of a therapeutic relationship. Check in with your provider regularly to ensure the work remains relevant to your goals.

How do I find a provider?

Finding a provider can feel overwhelming, and it's hard to know where to start. The most important part is finding someone who is a good match for you. Referrals from trusted providers and reviewing therapist profiles can be a helpful starting point. You can also use directories like Psychology Today, Zencare, or Zocdoc to filter by location, insurance, and specialty.

Once you find someone who seems like a fit, schedule a free consultation if they offer one. That conversation can give you a sense of their style and help you decide whether to schedule a first session. It often takes several sessions to see if the sessions are effective so be patient with the process. Taking the first step toward counseling takes courage, and you already started. You deserve to find someone who truly gets you.

If you'd like to learn more about my experience and see if we are a good match, read on below.

A Foundation of Clinical Expertise

In the world of mental health, experience matters. My experience spans more than 25 years across hospitals, community mental health, and digital health platforms, as well as supporting professionals at Universities, Big Tech, and Fortune 500 companies. I also teach as an adjunct professor at Boston College, where I help train the next generation of clinicians. This breadth of clinical work means I understand the challenges that individuals go through and the pressures of your environment. My role is to be a steady anchor drawing on my years of seeing human resilience and growth in action.

My Clinical Approach: Compassionate and Practical

I believe that counseling should be more than just "talking." It should be an active, collaborative process that gives you tangible results. My practice blends warm, respectful, culturally sensitive care. As a Taiwanese American clinician, I understand the importance of holding space for your full identity; including cultural background, family values, and the unique ways those shape your experience. I integrate your experiences with evidence based clinical interventions.

We won't just talk about how you feel; we will work together to understand the patterns of your thoughts, emotions, and behaviors. I draw from a range of therapeutic approaches, but what matters most is how they are helpful in a session.

Therapeutic Approaches:

  • Humanistic & Person-Centered: A space where you are truly heard and respected. You are the expert on your life; I’m here to provide the empathy and support to help you grow.
  • Strengths-Based: Shifting the focus from what feels "broken" to what is already working. We identify the resources, resilience, and skills you already possess, using them as the foundation for navigating challenges.
  • Motivational Interviewing: A collaborative conversation that helps you connect with your own "why" and strengthens your resolve and confidence to make a change.
  • Existential Approach: Exploring the "big questions" of life related to meaning, purpose, and values.
  • Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT): Understanding the connection between your thoughts, feelings, and behaviors so you can break the cycles that keep you stuck.
  • Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT): Building flexibility so you can stop fighting unwanted thoughts and emotions and take action on what matters.
  • Solution-Focused: Instead of staying stuck in the problem, we look at practical steps that will get you to where you want to be.
  • Mindfulness: Simple tools to help you quiet the noise, lower the stress, and find a bit of calm in the middle of a busy day.

Do you take insurance?

I do not take insurance. Depending on your insurance plan, you may have out of network benefits. I can provide you with a "super bill" that you can submit for potential reimbursement.

Where I am Licensed to Practice

It is important to be clear about the legal and ethical boundaries of clinical counseling. Unlike coaching, which can be done globally, counseling is regulated by state licensing boards to ensure quality of care.

I am currently licensed to provide clinical counseling to individuals residing in the following locations only:

  • Massachusetts (MA)
  • Connecticut (CT)
  • New Hampshire (NH)
  • Maine (ME)
  • Vermont (VT)
  • Washington, D.C. (DC)

If you live outside of these areas and need counseling services, start by looking for a therapist who is licensed in your state and has experience with what you're dealing with, whether that is anxiety, depression, burnout, relationship stress, or a major life challenge. Read their profile to get a feel for how they work, because style and approach really do matter. Most therapists also offer a free consultation, and I always encourage people to use that time to ask questions and see if the fit feels right.

Ready to Begin?

Schedule your free 15 minute consultation

title: Life Transition Coaching: Navigating the Internal Shift with Transitional Intelligence
author: Alice Kim, LICSW
content:
Sometimes, there is a catalyst that starts the process of reevaluating where you are in your current season. Maybe it is a life event, a milestone, or a timely conversation. Other times, it’s a realization that your life no longer fits who you are becoming. These moments can feel both vulnerable and important.

Clinically Informed Coaching: Integrating Clinical Expertise with Life Transitions

There is a meaningful difference between Life Coaching and Clinically Informed Coaching. Coaching is designed to help you move forward with partnership and support. My background as a Licensed Independent Clinical Social Worker (LICSW) adds another layer: an understanding of human behavior, emotional patterns, stress responses, and the internal blocks that can keep you stuck.

I have years of experience applying this knowledge and translating them into skills, providing therapy to hundreds of people in their own journeys. Not only do I practice it, I also teach it to undergraduate and graduate students as an adjunct professor at Boston College. I've spent over two decades supporting individuals from diverse cultures and backgrounds across a wide range of environments, including healthcare, higher education, Big Tech, and Fortune 500 companies.

I offer Clinically Informed Coaching, which means I bring a clinical foundation built over 25+ years of experience and my role as an educator, into a future-oriented, action-focused coaching process. My approach is not just about making a plan. It is about understanding the internal process of transition so you can grow through it, not just get through it.

As a TQ (Transitional Intelligence) Certified Coach, I integrate my own lived experiences into my work as a coach. I've experienced multiple life transitions myself including immigrating as a young child, navigating multiple ethnic identities as an Asian American, career pivots, parenting and empty nesting, and entering my own second half of life. Together, these perspectives help me support you in moving through your seasons of change with clarity and a grounded understanding of transformation.

Research-Backed Approaches:

Together, we will use practical, research-backed tools to support resilience:

  • Strengths-Based: We identify the internal resources you already possess. We lead with your resilience and build on what is already working.
  • Mindfulness-Informed: Uncertainty can activate stress quickly. We develop the capacity to stay grounded and present through the ups and downs.
  • Solution-Focused: We create a path aligned with your values and your "why."
  • Action with Awareness: We ensure your next steps are thoughtful, and actionable.

What to Expect in Our Sessions

  • Exploring identity: Discovering who you are today and who you want to be in this next chapter.
  • Clarifying what matters: Identifying your core values and the life that feels aligned now.
  • Building your TQ: Identifying where you are in the transition process and building skills to navigate them.
  • Working with internal blocks: Noticing patterns like over functioning, self-doubt, or fear of uncertainty and developing new mindsets and patterns.
  • Taking thoughtful action: Translating insight into practical next steps that are realistic and sustainable.

Who is This For?

Life Transition Coaching is designed for individuals who are forward thinking, ready to take purposeful steps toward a fuller and meaningful life. You might find yourself in one of these "seasons":

  • Major Life Milestones: From graduating and marriage to becoming a parent or an empty nester, these shifts require a recalibration of your identity and your daily rhythms.
  • Career Pivots: You’ve reached a level of success in your field, but you’re wondering if there’s a new path that feels more authentic to who you are today.
  • The Transition to Retirement: Often, we plan for the finances of retirement but forget the inner work. I help you navigate the shift from "doing" to "being" and redefine your purpose.
  • Rewriting the Script: You are ready to build and take responsibility for your life rather than live a life designed by others’ expectations.

Global Access

Life’s transitions don't stop at borders. Whether you’re a recent graduate starting a first job in Seoul, a parent navigating a move to London, redefining your purpose in San Francisco, or retiring in Costa Rica, Life transition coaching meets you where you are. One of the most liberating aspects of coaching is its accessibility and availability to you regardless of where you live.

Our work happens entirely online, making Clinically Informed Life Transitions Coaching accessible from any time zone. I work with clients around the US, Europe, Asia, and beyond to navigate change with intention.

Investment and Structure

Coaching is an investment in your future self. It is a dedicated space where you are the central focus, and the goal is tangible, meaningful change. I offer structured coaching packages designed to provide the consistency and momentum needed for meaningful change.

Schedule your free 15 minute consultation. Click here for coaching packages.

Written by Alice Kim, LICSW with AI assistance. Photographs in this post are original works by Alice Kim

title: Finding your Way: A Map for Navigating Life’s Transitions
author: Alice Kim, LICSW
content:
Have you ever found that you just entered a major milestone— graduation, a promotion, retirement, or even the birth of a child — and instead of the expected feeling of satisfaction, you feel a sense of disorientation, or even a quiet grief? Or have you experienced living a life that looks exactly the way you planned, yet feeling like a stranger within it? Or, perhaps more commonly, life did not go the way you hoped; you experienced a curve ball and feel like you’ve lost your footing?

Whatever the catalyst, these moments share a common thread: they signal that the ground beneath you has shifted. If you find yourself overwhelmed, stuck, or simply asking, "What happened and what now?", you are likely in the middle of a life transition. One helpful distinction is understanding the difference between a change and a transition. A change is the event; a transition is the internal process.

Change vs. Transition:

Change is external and can be fast. It can be the event such as the move, the diagnosis, the layoff, the breakup, empty nesting. Transition is internal and usually slower. It asks questions like “What is important? What are my values? Who am I? Who do I want to be?” It’s the psychological integration that follows.

That distinction matters because many people assume that once the event is over, they should already feel settled. But the mind and heart often needs much longer to reorganize around a loss, expectation, new role, or identity.

The Phases of a Transition:

William Bridges offers a map to understanding life's changes. Transitions begin with an ending, move through a neutral zone, and eventually open into a new beginning. That framework helps explain why life can feel unsettled internally even after something has already changed externally. A change can happen quickly, but the internal adjustment takes more time than we anticipated. There is no set timeline for this inner process. Bruce Feiler adds another important perspective: transitions are often nonlinear. He describes major disruptions as "lifequakes"—events like illness, divorce, caregiving, job loss, or relocation that shake the structure of your life. A lifequake may happen suddenly, but the transition that follows is usually longer, messier, and less predictable than people expect.

The Ending: Letting Go

Every transition begins with some form of ending, a loss. You may be leaving behind a role, a routine, a relationship, or an identity. Even welcome changes can carry grief, anxiety, or disorientation because something familiar is ending.

The Neutral Zone: The Messy Middle

This is the space between the "no longer" and the "not yet." It often feels foggy, unproductive, or emotionally confusing, but it is also where deeper reflection happens even when it does not feel good.

The New Beginning

Over time, a new beginning starts to take shape. You are not just managing the change; you are building new patterns, meanings, and ways of being. You integrate your past with your present, evolving sense of self.

Transitional Intelligence (TQ)

To navigate these phases effectively, it helps to build what Kari Cardinale calls "Transitional Intelligence" (TQ). Cardinale has coined the term “Transitional Intelligence” as “the capacity to perceive life’s transitions as internal opportunities for growth, to recognize the stage you are in, and to skillfully move through it with compassion, clarity, and confidence.” She describes how these skills can help you move through change with greater steadiness, self-awareness, and intention.

Instead of treating transition like a problem to solve quickly, TQ focuses on your ability to notice what is ending, tolerate uncertainty, revise old assumptions, and lean into new growth and integrate it over time. This framework is useful because transitions are not just logistical. They are also emotional, relational, cognitive, and existential.

Coaching vs. Therapy

Transitions can be navigated and supported through self-reflection, community, and time. Having a coach can also offer partnership, enhance your understanding of your experience, and assist with identifying and moving you towards your values and goals. Other transitions can benefit from extra support with a therapist if it is activating anxiety, depression, trauma responses, prolonged grief, relationship strain, or a strong sense of being emotionally stuck.

Final Thoughts

Life transitions is not just a change in circumstances. Separating change from transition gives you a more nuanced map of your experience. It helps explain why some shifts feel manageable and others feel seismic.

When you focus only on the external event, it is easy to judge yourself for still feeling unsettled. But anxiety, grief, confusion, or fatigue are often a normal part of the process. Instead of asking, "Why am I not over this yet?," a more useful question may be, "What is this transition asking me to let go of, hold on to, deal with, or learn?"

Transition is an internal process of reflection, reorganization, edits, and identity evolution. Bridges names the phases, Feiler reminds us that major shifts can arrive as nonlinear lifequakes, and TQ reframes these seasons as an opportunity for revision, wisdom, and a new sense of purpose. If you are in the middle of a major shift, uncertainty is not a sign of failure. Often, it is a sign that something important is taking shape.

Sources & Further Reading

  • William Bridges — Transitions: Making Sense of Life’s Changes
  • Bruce Feiler — Life Is in the Transitions
  • https://www.meawisdom.com/ebook/the-anatomy-of-a-transition/

Written by Alice Kim, LICSW with AI assistance. Photographs in this post are original works by Alice Kim
